meta.title: 2.2 The Box Model
meta.description: Every element on a webpage is a box. Margin, border, padding, content — the four layers.

blocks.prompt: When you see a button on a webpage with space around it and space between the text and the edges of the button, where do you think that spacing comes from? How many different types of spacing do you think there are?

blocks.question: If a developer says 'there is too much margin on that card,' could you explain what margin is and where to look for it in the CSS?
blocks.answer: Margin is the space outside an element's border — the gap between it and its neighbors. You would look for `margin`, `margin-top`, `margin-bottom`, `margin-left`, or `margin-right` properties on that element's CSS rule. You could also right-click the element, choose Inspect, and check the box model diagram to see the exact margin values.

problems.explanation: The total space includes all four layers on both sides: margin (15) + border (5) + padding (20) + content (300) + padding (20) + border (5) + margin (15) = 380px. This is why things sometimes look bigger than the `width` value suggests.

problems.question: A button has text that feels cramped — the text is pressed right up against the edges of the button. Which CSS property would fix this?
options: A) `margin` — add space around the button
B) `padding` — add space inside the button, between the text and the edge
C) `border` — make the button edges thicker
problems.explanation: Padding is the space inside the element between the content and the border. If the text feels cramped inside a button, adding padding gives the text more breathing room. Margin would add space outside the button (between the button and other elements), which is a different problem.

problems.question: There is too much vertical space between sections on a page. You want to tell AI to fix it. What is the most precise way to describe the problem?
options: B) "Reduce the margin-bottom on the section elements — there is too much space between them"
C) "Delete the blank lines between the sections in the HTML"
problems.explanation: The most precise instruction references the specific property causing the problem. Space between elements is controlled by margin. Saying "reduce the margin-bottom on sections" tells AI exactly what to change. Option A is vague, and option C is wrong — spacing between elements comes from CSS, not blank lines in HTML.

blocks.title: Inspect the box model on a real webpage. Open any website (your company's site, a news site, anything), right-click on a heading or button, and select 'Inspect.' Find the box model diagram in the panel and identify the content, padding, border, and margin values. What surprised you about the spacing?
blocks.solution: You should see a nested rectangle diagram with four layers showing pixel values. Most elements have more margin and padding than you might expect — that is normal. You may also notice that some values are 0 while others are quite large. The box model diagram is color-coded: content (blue), padding (green), border (yellow/brown), and margin (orange).

blocks.content: Every element is a box with four layers: content, padding, border, and margin. Padding is space inside the box (between content and border). Margin is space outside the box (between the element and its neighbors). When spacing looks wrong, inspect the element to see its box model. Use precise language — 'reduce the padding' or 'increase the margin' — when describing spacing issues to AI.

blocks.prompt: You now know how individual boxes are sized and spaced. But how do boxes arrange themselves on the page? Why do some things sit side by side while others stack? Module 2.3 covers layout — the rules that determine where every box ends up.

reviewQuestions.question: In the CSS box model, every element has four layers. From inside to outside, they are:
reviewQuestions.explanation: The box model layers from inside out: content (the text/image), padding (space inside the border), border (the edge), margin (space outside the border).
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Perfect! Content, padding, border, margin — inside to outside.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The box model from inside to outside: content, padding, border, margin. Padding is inside the box, margin is outside.

reviewQuestions.question: Padding is space:
options: Outside the element, between it and its neighbors
Inside the element, between the content and the border
Only above and below the element
reviewQuestions.explanation: Padding is inside the box — the space between the content and the border. Margin is outside the box — the space between the element and its neighbors.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's the key distinction! Padding is inside, margin is outside.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Padding is the space inside the box (between content and border). Margin is the space outside the box (between the element and its neighbors).
